Wang et al. Infectious Disease Modelling 11 (2026) 947–957 1. Introduction Acute respiratory infectious diseases are characterized by distinct symptoms and rapid transmissibility. In a brief period, a large number of patients can emerge. Under certain circumstances, these diseases can even cross national and continental boundaries, giving rise to a global pandemic (Baker et al., 2022; Guan et al., 2004). Consequently, the demand for medical resources experiences a dramatic upsurge, thereby precipitating a medical exigency that exerts a deleterious impact on the global public health security landscape. Simultaneously, it has imposed significant constraints on people's routine activities and mobility patterns (Kim, 2020; Mao et al., 2020; Waterer, 2023). Early-phase outbreaks of novel respiratory pathogens exhibit elevated case fatality rates, attributable to viral patho- genicity, immunological naivety as well as healthcare system strain. Between November 2002 and August 2003, the average mortality rate of Severe acute respiratory syndrome(SARS) cases reported by 29 countries is 9.3% (Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention). According to the World Health Organization(WHO), it was the first severe and readily transmissible new disease to emerge in the 21st century and showed a clear capacity to spread along the routes of international air travel (World Health Organization, 2003). Approximately 35% of Middle East Respiratory Syndrome(MERS) cases(firstly discov- ered in 2012) reported to WHO have died (World Health Organization, 2022). In March 2020, the World Health Organization announced that Corona Virus Disease 2019(COVID-19) had entered a pandemic state. As of the end of March 2020, the fatality rate of COVID-19 was 4.8%, according to data released by the WHO (World Health Organization, 2020). The main factor that drives the spread of COVID-19 is closeness between an infected individual and a vulnerable indi- vidual. Close-contact gatherings frequently trigger superspreading events, characterized by disproportionate secondary transmission rates. The exponential growth of international air travel has become a critical accelerator of cross-border disease transmission (Hertzberg & Weiss, 2016; Mangili et al., 2015; Namilae et al., 2022; Shingleton et al., 2023). Modeling studies demonstrate an significant correlation between changes in the rate of spread and yearly fluctuations in monthly airline volume (Brownstein et al., 2006), with similar patterns observed in COVID-19 outbreaks (Chinazzi et al., 2020). Early detection of infected inbound travelers through airport screening reduces secondary transmission risk. Epidemiological investigations consistently identify travel history and close contact exposure as significant predictors of infection risk during outbreaks, forming the basis of case definitions for diseases like MERS and COVID-19 (Duong & Waldman, 2016; Hon, 2013; Li et al., 2021).This study developed a predictive model for assessing COVID-19 infection risk among international arrivals by integrating traveler-specific data(detailed travel history (14-day country-specific residence), light itineraries (including aircraft seating configurations and transit duration) and epidemiological intelligence(WHO- reported country-level COVID-19 new cases), and investigated methodologies for quantifying travel-associated risk and delineating close contacts boundaries. We hope that this predictive model, when combined with antibody or viral nucleic acid screening methods, can classify the infection risk of inbound travelers, thereby slowing the rate of interpersonal virus transmission. Our study primarily consists of three components: 1) quantifying travel-associated risk and delineating close contacts boundaries, 2) establishing an infection prediction model, and 3) examining the epidemiological significance of key variables in the model. 2. Ac- Satellite remote sensing curate prediction of the optimal time for harvesting therefore represents a key precautionary Microwave SAR countermeasure that can mitigate against the damage caused by heat exposure and excessive Rice grain heat-induced grain dryness. The remote sensing of grain moisture content, which is an important Vegetation water content indicator for accurate harvest timings, has been rarely studied using microwave Synthetic Panicle Aperture Radar (SAR). Here we examine the relationship between crop biophysical parameters and C-band SAR backscatter in order to determine the correlation between field-measured grain moisture content and satellite SAR backscatter. This study takes advantage of the specific prop- erties of microwave radar signals, and, in particular, their sensitivity to moisture content. We first pre-processed Sentinel-1 data taken in 2020 and 2022 to extract radar backscatter values aver- aged within rice crop fields. Partial least squares regression was used to determine the relation between field-measured crop biophysical parameters gathered over a 2-year period and back- scatter data acquired during the ripening stage. This statistical analysis revealed that co-polarized radar backscatter strengths obtained with shallow incidence angles (of around 45◦) exhibited a significant correlation to grain moisture content and leaf and tiller moisture content (R2 =0.669), with grain moisture showing the highest variable importance. On the other hand, the correlation with these biophysical parameters was not statistically significant for backscatter at the steeper incidence angle (of around 35◦). The ripening phenomenon restricts stem elongation in order to furnish nutrients to the grain so that the physical structure of the plant changes little and grain moisture substantially decreases. This most likely explains why backscatter signals obtained at shallower incidence angles, which are more susceptible to interaction with the crop canopy layer, can be strongly affected by grains. Our study indicates the great potential of synoptic C-band SAR observations for the estimation of grain moisture content in rice within individual rice-crop fields and thus points the way toward improved prediction of optimal harvest timings. These results should eventually lead to a better adaptation of rice cultivation to climate change and hence contribute to the establishment of more stable rice supplies. * Corresponding author. Introduction Rising temperatures due to global warming can damage rice crops, thereby reducing yields and lowering grain quality (Takimoto et al., 2019). These changes are significant because rice is a staple food for more than half of the world's population (Shi et al., 2023). It is projected that global rice consumption will continue to rise (Durand-Morat and Mulimbi, 2025) and that 75% of this rice production will be directly consumed as food by humans rather than used as livestock feed (OECD-FAO, 2025). Most rice is grown in Asian countries (Fukagawa and Ziska, 2019), and Japan, for example, reaches almost 100% self-sufficiency in the production of rice of the Oryza sativa L. variety (MAFF, 2024a). While Japan has not so far experienced significant yield loss (e-Stat, 2024), decreased grain quality due to heat damage is confidently predicted and is deeply concerning because rice quality is an important factor in the Japanese market. The Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries of Japan (MAFF) reported that, due to abnormally hot weather in 2023, only 60.9% of the rice grown in that year could be considered first class, which was the lowest on record since current inspection procedures began in 2004 (MAFF, 2024b). In addition, the percentages of first-class rice grown in 11 prefectures mainly in the northern part of Japan fell by more than 10% compared to values in 2022. In Japan, the rice variety “Koshihikari” has the highest reputation due to its quality and palatability and is thus the most produced cultivar (33.1% of total production); however, its low heat tolerance makes it vulnerable to climate change (Ishimaru et al., 2022). In order to minimize high-temperature damage, which typically results in chalkiness and cracked, immature grains, several countermeasures have been introduced at different growth stages. Some progress toward a fundamental solution of these problems involving improvements to existing cultivars and the development of new varieties with enhanced heat tolerance has been reported (Masutomi et al., 2023). However, immediate practical countermeasures such as delayed transplantation, adjustments to water and fertilizer application, free-flow irrigation, postponement of drainage periods, and harvesting with optimal timing are required (Morita et al., 2016). The selection of an optimal timing for each harvested field can serve as an important final countermeasure and is thus essential for an appropriate harvesting strategy. Although local governments publish estimates of optimal harvest timings (Sakaiya and Inoue, 2013) these estimates are nevertheless made at municipal level and based on field observations of heading date and accu- mulated daily average temperature obtained at a limited number of sample plots. Their applicability to individual fields that may experience different management practices and growth may be limited. Grain moisture content (GMC) can be considered as a key parameter for determining an optimal timing because when harvest is delayed excessively the rice plants are exposed to high tem- peratures for longer and the consequent loss of moisture leads to lower-quality grains. Thus, measurements of GMC at the field level are required for improved guidance to ensure optimal harvest timing. More direct measurements of GMC, specific to individual fields, can be achieved by remote sensing technology. Recently, Un- manned Aircraft Systems (UAS) operating at high spatial resolution have become more popular for agricultural use. In the present context, the application of such multispectral remote sensing techniques for GMC estimation is significant (Yang et al., 2025). However, although these systems are able to collect data at high spectral and spatial resolution, the observational area they cover must be limited in order to compensate for the high resolution of the imagery. On the other hand, satellite remote sensing offers considerably wider-swath spatial coverage and has recently been employed for short-term crop monitoring following recent improvements in both spatial resolution and acquisition frequency. Future technological advances will only enhance the trend towards satellite-based ap- proaches. Data from optical satellites have been used in agricultural applications (Fang et al., 2024), and frequent observations of the type required for crop monitoring have been acquired by the Sentinel-2 satellite. Imagery collected in the near infrared band by Sentinel-2 have been used to forecast GMC (Brinkhoff et al., 2025; Jha et al., 2025). However, the use of visible band and near infrared imaging technology is unsuitable in cases where persistent cloud cover obscures the Earth's surface, such as in regions of rice culti- vation which typically require plentiful rainfall. In contrast, active satellite synthetic aperture radar (SAR) can be applied to rice crop monitoring because it leverages the distinct properties of an all-weather sensor and operates by transmitting microwave signals down onto the Earth's surface and recording the subsequent backscatter signals. A relatively higher image acquisition repeat frequency and the free-data policy employed by the European Space Agency (ESA) with regard to the distribution of Sentinel-1 data have both greatly facilitated SAR data utilization in agricultural research applications. Previous SAR-based studies in crop growth analysis generally fall into two categories: (1) tracking seasonal changes in SAR backscatter in relation to crop growth (Erten et al., 2015; Fikriyah et al., 2025; Khabbazan et al., 2019; Veloso et al., 2017) and (2) comparing SAR backscatter directly to field observations of biophysical parameters (Harfenmeister et al., 2019; Kobayashi and Ide, 2022; Kushwaha et al., 2022; Vreugdenhil et al., 2018). As the former method has the advantage of being able to define the general backscattering trend over larger crop areas, work in this research category is extensive. In contrast, the latter method employing in situ observation is clearly necessary for a robust understanding of the parameters that affect SAR backscatter. Nevertheless, case studies have been limited and generally use several basic measurements such as crop height and biomass. Only a few attempts have so far been made to investigate moisture content in plants, even though it is well known that microwave backscatter is sensitive not only to vegetation structure but also to the moisture content of the vegetation (Henderson and Lewis, 1998; Khabbazan et al., 2019; Kobayashi et al., 2024). Harfenmeister et al. (2019) discovered that C-band backscatter increases with decreasing vegetation moisture content (VMC) in wheat and barley. Kushwaha et al. (2022)studied the relationship between VMC and C-band SAR data in rice crops; however, a significant correlation between these parameters was not demonstrated during the ripening stage. It should be noted that these previous studies used VMC values representative of the whole plant body and did not separately measure GMC, which is the crucial biophysical parameter that largely determines optimal harvest timing. Notably, Harfenmeister et al. (2019)suggested the importance of the separate measurement of GMC. In summary, besides the limitations of UAS and optical satellite applications, previous SAR studies have mainly analyzed seasonal changes over extensive areas without involving field measurements. Phased dynamic analysis and prediction o.authors: JournalofIntegrativeAgriculture Phased dynamic analysis and prediction of rice Chilo suppressalis integrating remote sensing physiological indices and environmental factors YuchenWu1,LihuaWang1,2#,YanxiaoBao1,WeiweiSun1,2,ZhiyuanYao3,GangYang1,2,YumiaoWang1,2 1SchoolofGeographyandRemotesensing,NingboUniversity,Ningbo315211,China 2 Ningbo Key Laboratory of Remote Sensing and Ecological Security of Coastal Zone, Zhejiang-Germany JointLaboratoryonRemoteSensingofCoastalEcosystem,NingboUniversity,Ningbo315211,China 3 Institute of One Health Science, School of Civil & Environmental Engineering and Geography Science, StateKeylaboratoryforQualityandSafetyofAgro-prouctsNingboUniversity.Ningbo315211,China Highlights  Introduced LOESS smoothing and first-derivative analysis to identify the key developmental stages of Chilosuppressalis.  Determinedthescreeningschemeforsensitivityparametersspecifictoeachpestdevelopmentstage.  Constructed a stage-specific daily dynamic prediction framework to achieve spatiotemporally explicit extrapolationofC.suppressalispopulationdynamicsattheregionalscale. Abstract Chilo suppressalis is a major pest in rice-producing regions, posing serious threats to rice yield and quality. Existing pest prediction research generally ignore the stage-specific heterogeneity in population dynamics and neglect the synergistic effects among meteorological, soil, and rice physiological information. This makes it challenging to accurately characterize the complete dynamic changes in pest populations. In this study, we explicitly highlight three methodological innovations: (1) the use of a LOESS-based curve fitting and slope-change detection framework to objectively partition C. suppressalis population dynamics into threestages—populationestablishment,expansion,andoutbreak;and(2)theintegrationofmulti-sourcedata, includingtrapmonitoring,ricephysiologicalindicesderivedfromremotesensing,andERA5meteorological and soil variables, to construct stage-specific prediction models.; and (3) building upon this stage-based framework, we designed a targeted sensitivity-parameter screening scheme and developed daily dynamic predictionmodelsusingtheRandomForest(RF)algorithm,whichincorporatemeteorological,soil,andcrop physiological indicators.The results demonstrate that the proposed stage-specific prediction model achieves excellent performance. During the outbreak stage, the R2 for all three experimental fields exceeds 0.9, with MAE below 23.16 and RMSE under 32.86. In the Jiulong field, stage-specific predictions show R2 values above 0.89. Compared with Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) and Prophet models, RF exhibits superior stability and generalization, with test set R² consistently above 0.69, highlighting its robustness and reliability for stage-specific prediction of C. suppressalis population dynamics. These findings highlight the practical value of our approach for enhancing comprehensive pest forecasting and supporting targeted pest management. The Food andAgriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) latest forecast predicts that global rice production is expected to reach 555.6 million tons (t) (FAO2025),anditsproductionstabilityisdirectlyrelatedtoglobalfoodsecurityandsustainableagricultural economies(Wangetal.2022).However,affectedbyfactorssuchasclimatechangeandtheintensificationof cultivation practices, global crop losses due to pest damage reach up to 40% annually, with economic losses exceeding $220 billion (Anonymous 2021), among which the yield gap caused by rice diseases and pests is particularly prominent. The National Agricultural Technology Extension and Service Center of China predicts that rice pest will generally occur severely in 2025, with an affected area of approximately 86.02 millionhectares(ha),ofwhichtheaffectedareaofriceChilosuppressaliswillbe16.67millionha(Liuetal. 2025). Chilo suppressalis was listed inChina's "CategoryI Crop Diseases and Pests Directory" in 2020andhas the characteristic of secondary occurrence, capable of causing continuous damage to rice at different growth stages (Ge et al. 2025). During the tillering stage, it causes dead leaf seedlings. During the booting stage, it forms dead heart seedlings. During the heading stage, it causes white panicles and lodging. This is particularly problematic in rice-growing areas where pesticide resistance is increasingly severe, such as China(Moetal.2025),Japan(Ishiguroetal.2007),andIran(Majidi-Shilsaretal.2023).Chilosuppressalis impedes water and nutrient transport by boring into rice stems and destroying vascular bundles, severely affecting normal rice growth,andcan lead to riceyield reductions of 30to50% (Gaoet al. 2020;Mao et al. 2022). In severely affected fields, total crop failure may even occur. Therefore, constructing accurate pest forecasting models not only provides scientific guidance to plant protection departments in formulating targeted control strategies, but also is a strategic requirement for achieving pest-resilient grain production andsafeguardingnationalfoodsecurity. As a major stem-boring pest of rice, C. suppressalis requires accurate prediction of its occurrence dynamics as a key component for achieving integrated pest management in agriculture. Current research on crop pest forecasting and early warning systems primarily focuses on assessment of annual overall risk (Huang et al. 2019; Makori et al. 2024), and short-term predictions of one week (Ibrahim et al. 2022; Chacón-Maldonado et al. 2025) or one month (Ma et al. 2019; Skawsang et al. 2019). These approaches typically analyze and model the entire pest occurrence period as a single, unified object. However, C. suppressalis exhibits significant stage-specific characteristics during its actual occurrence process, with pest population showing multi-peak fluctuations in time series, where each peak corresponds to the generational succession process. Current UAV remote sensing yield assessment relies on destructive sampling, which limits spatial and temporal resolution. While UAV-based Yield prediction remote sensing is extensively used for cereal yield prediction, quinoa applications remain largely limited to Machine learning process-based modeling, representing a critical knowledge gap. This study presents the first demonstration of 3D UAV-based, plant-level yield prediction for quinoa using machine learning and 3D structural features. Unlike Growth stage Precision agriculture major cereals, quinoa exhibits highly diverse canopy colors and complex morphology across genotypes and growth stages, making spectral vegetation indices unreliable. Therefore, 3D structural features provide a more robust basis for yield prediction by integrating branching complexity and canopy density, which directly relate to biomass accumulation and reproductive capacity. We conducted UAV surveys combined with machine learning across five growth stages over two seasons (2022 & 2024–2025). RGB and multispectral imagery generated 16 features, including 3D structural parameters and vegetation indices. Five ML regression models were evaluated at each stage, with Random Forest at flowering achieving optimal accuracy (R² =0.726, RMSE =16.71 g/m²). Early and late vegetative stages demonstrated slightly lower but still robust prediction accuracy (R² = 0.690–0.704), enabling practical early-season management decisions. Notably, 3D structural features, particu- larly plant volume and surface area, substantially outperformed spectral indices. These results validate UAV- based ML for non-destructive quinoa yield prediction and demonstrate the essential role of 3D structural fea- tures for architecturally complex crops. 1. Using UAV multispectral data, 69 vegetation Multi-temporal data indices (VIs) were constructed and evaluated for three rice cultivars (Ningxiangjing9, Taian1, and Yinxiang38) under varying nitrogen treatments, and the optimal VIs were selected for five rice growth stages. Ten meteo- rological features (MF) per growth stage, including temperature and precipitation, were integrated into rice yield prediction models to account for environmental influences. In evaluating the prediction of rice yield, six regression models were assessed: simple linear regression (SLR), partial least squares regression (PLSR), random forest (RF), gaussian process regression (GPR), support vector machine (SVM), and extreme gradient boosting (XGB).
